annotations.content: My name is Cathy Didilokadinr(?) I'm a poet and I'm also the director for Jodigum which is a none profit based here in Mediro.

People aren't talking about climate change every single day, people are living their lives. They are worried about, you know, bills, and picking up their kids, and family parties. Things like that. And yet there are ways in which it's impacting the land, it's changing the terrain of our land and it's changing the way we plan and we build our houses and the ways in which we prepare for our future.

I saw the research from Jim Fletcher, you know, telling us basically 1.5 has been passed and there's is nothing we can do about that and that we need to abandon, you know, the hope that we can still maintain 1.5 at all. And um, 1.5 is supposed to mean survival for us.

My name is Jimmy Langley, I work at the national desaster management office as the director. We used to have a big beautiful beach, 'Oratora'(?), but right now it's kind of depleating, I would say. Because, the beaches that we witnessed, I witnessed as a kid, it's getting smaller. And I am not a scientift per se but I witnessed with my own eyes as growing up, in Maduro (town?).
I don't see the palm tree that I used to swing into the water.

There used to be lots of areas that used to be there but are not there. There used to be houses and graveyards on the ocean site that are not there. So, you know, we have people that say climate change is not real. And um... it's kind of disheartening and sad when you hear people say that because they come from places where they are not affected by it.

Hello! ('yawey' in marshallese) My name is Angely Hineramus. I work for the people (...) the Marshall Island's government as the national energy planner. If you go to the Marshall Island's high school uh, right across at the lagoon site, at the beach, there used to be an area where there was lots of pandanas (?) trees and um, benches for students to you know sit and gather. That area is no longer there. That area was there in 1993 when I graduated from high school.

My name is John Filippo. And I work at the Molare (?) a cooperate processing (?). I have my own property and I'm looking everyday giving away. The land being eaten and .... what is really climate change.

Hello everyone! ... (in marshallese) All along the beach here where my property, my water is, I can see the erosion that is taking place, you know, cutting, eroding a lot of the beach sand. So in 20 years people are telling eyesm you know. It's happening faster than we think.

(speaking marshallese) I work as a e-bike project representative for the prep 2 project. My house is situated on ocean site. And I remember as a child I would wake up and would always run towards the ocean. I would climb the slope down you know, just go down the slope from rocks to sand and then to the beach reef. But today, when my sibblings would want to go to swim, I would walk them down and everytime I'd go back I I'd be in shock. I'd stand there for severy seconds just saying what happend? Before you would climb down rocks and then walk on sand and then reach the reef and now you're climbing down rocks and then reef. Right there. So it's very scarry. And, late at night, when you feel the strong wind and high waves, strong waves, you feel it much closer.  You feel the vibrations of the power, the strength of these waves just you know that force just... not physically hitting your house but the sound of it, it's much closer to your own home and it's a very scary thought, especially when you have children around you.

My name is Don Hess. I came out to the Marshall islands to teach. When I first came here the inodations.. very rare! You know, once every fice years, 3-5 years, you might have some inodations. In the last 5-10 years, 2-3 times a year maybe. The water lenses get inodated, get flooded with salt water intrusion. You see a lot of the cemeteries that are the gravesites that are near the cost. The land starting to erode and ? are starting to fall in, the trees are starting to fall in along the cost lines, some of the cost lines, which wasn't that way when I first got here.
When the water comes into the land, salt intrusion especially for the water wells, for the well water, can sometime affect the.. a household or two because they depend so much on the well.

My name is Eunice Borero and I am the electronic reporting officer in Mimra, it's the Marshall island's marine resources authority. Everyone is feeling it, they are loosing homes, everyone is talking now about putting sea walls up. It's really gonna make the island different.

The ocean, the lagoons are the providers for pacific islanders, I mean, people here will say the lagoon is their ice box. You know they just go out and fish and that's their, their grocery, their supermarket is in the middle of the lagoon, just get out there with your fishing line and hook.
It is a hard battle for everyone because... we only have the water but if the water is going to be your enemy.... what's gonna happen?
